Okay, yeah, thanks for joining me. I'm still on the West Coast time, so it feels like I'm doing this at like 7 a.m. So yeah, but glad to talk to you about the Claude Agent SDK.

So yeah, I think this is going to be like a rough agenda of what we're going to talk about. We're going to talk about like, what is the Claude Agent SDK? Why use it? There's so many other agent frameworks. What is an agent? What is the agent framework? How do you design an agent using the Agent SDK, or just in general?

And then I'm going to do some like live coding, or Claude is going to do some live coding on prototyping an agent, and I've got some starter code. But yeah, the whole goal of this is like,

we've got two hours, we're going to be super collaborative, ask questions. This is also going to be not like a super canned demo, in the sense that like, we're going to be like thinking through things live, you know, I'm not going to have all the answers right away. And I think that'll be a good way of like, building an agent loop, I think is like really very much like kind of an art or intuition.

So yeah, before we get started, just curious, a show of hands, like how many people have heard of the Claude Agent SDK? Okay, great. Cool. How many of you have used it or tried it out? Okay, awesome. Okay, so pretty good show of hands. Yes, I'll just get started on like, the overview on agents.

I think that like, this is, I think something that people have seen before, but I think it's still, it's taking some time to like really think in how AI features are evolving, you know.

So I think like, when GPT-3 came out, it was really about like single LLM features, right? You're like, "Oh, like, hey, can you categorize this, like return a response in one of these categories? " And then we've got more like workflow-like things, right? "Hey, like, can you like, take this email and label it?

" or "like, hey, here's my code base, like index for your RAG, can you give me like the next completion or the next file to edit," right?

And so that's what we call like a workflow where you're very like, structured — you're like, "Hey, like, given this code, give me code back out," right? And now we're getting to agents, right? And like, the canonical agent is called code, right? Cloud Code is a tool where you don't really tell it, we don't restrict what it can do really, right?

You're just talking to it in text, and it will take a really wide variety of actions, right?

And so agents build their own context, like decide their own trajectories, are working very, very autonomously, right? And so — and so yeah, and I think like, as the future goes on, like agents will get more and more autonomous. And I think it's like, we're kind of at a great point where we can start to build these agents. these agents.

They're not perfect, you know, but it's definitely like the right time to get started.

So yeah, Cloud Code, I'm sure many of you have tried or use it. Yeah, I think the first true agent, right? Like the first time where I saw an AI working for like 10, 20, 30 minutes, right? So yeah, it's a coding agent. And the Claude Agent SDK is actually built on top of Cloud Code.

And the reason we did that is because basically, we found that when we were building agents at Anthropic, we kept rebuilding the same parts over and over again.

And so to give you a sense of like, what that looks like, of course, there are the models to start, right? And then in the harness, you've got tools, right? And that's like, sort of the first obvious step, like, let's add some tools to this harness.

And later on, we'll give an example of sort of, like trying to build your own harness from scratch too and what that looks like, and how challenging it can be.

But tools are not just like your own custom tools, they might be tools to attach to the file system, like with Cloud Code. Did the volume just go up? Or were they not holding it close enough? Okay, so I'm not — Cloud Code. Did the volume just go up? Or were they not holding it close enough?

Okay, so I'm not going to have it anyways, um, got tools, tools, you run in a loop, and then you have the prompts, right?

Like the core agent prompts, the prompts, things like that. And then finally, you have the file system, right? And we're not fine, but you have the file system; the file system is a way of context engineering that we'll talk more about later, right?

And I think like, one of the key insights we had through Cloud Code was thinking a lot more through the like context, not just a prompt — it's also the tools, the files, the scripts that it can use.

And then there are skills, which we've like rolled out recently. And we can talk more about skills, if that's interesting to you guys as well. And then yeah, things like sub-agents, web search, you know, like research, compacting hooks, memory — there are all these like other things around the harness as well. And it ends up being quite a lot.

So the Claude Agent SDK is all of these things packaged up for you to use. And yeah, you have your application.

So I think like, to give you a sense of, yeah, to give you a sense of like, maybe why the Claude Agent SDK is — like, so yeah, people are really building agents on the SDK: a lot of software agents, you know, software reliability, security, and then triaging, bug finding, site and dashboard builders — those are extremely popular. If you're using it, you should absolutely use SDK.

And it's office agents, if you're doing any sort of office work, tons of examples there. Got some like, you know, legal, finance, healthcare ones. So yeah, there are tons of people building on top of it.

I want to, but yeah, okay. So why the Claude Agent SDK, right? Like, why did we do it this way? Why do we build on top of Cloud Code? And we realized basically that as soon as we put Cloud Code out, yeah, the engineers started using it. But then the finance people started using it. And the data science people started using it. And the marketing people started using it.

And yeah, I think it's just like, we just realized that people were using Cloud Code for non-coding tasks. And we felt, and as we were building, you know, non-coding agents, we kept coming back to it.

And so it's like, and we'll go more into why that just works, why we continue to use Cloud Code for non-coding tasks. Spoiler alert, it's like the bash tool. But yeah, it was something that we saw as an emergent pattern that we want to use and we've built our agents on top of it. Right. And these are lessons that we've learned from deploying Cloud Code that we've sort of baked in. baked in.

So tool use errors or compacting or things like that — stuff that is like very, can take a lot of scale to find, you know, like what are the best practices we've sort of baked into the Claude Agent SDK.

As a result, we have a lot of strong opinions on the best way to build agents. Like I think the Claude Agent SDK is quite opinionated. I'll talk over some of these opinions and why, like why we chose them. Right. But yeah, one of the big opinions is that the bash tool is the most powerful agent tool.

So, okay. What, what are like, what I would describe as the Anthropic way to build agents, right? And I'm not saying that you can only build agents using the API this way. Right. But this is like, if you're using our opinionated stack on the Agent SDK, what is it, right? So roughly Unix primitives, like the bash and file system.

And, you know, we're going to go over like prototyping an agent using Cloud Code. And my goal is really to sort of show you what that looks like in real time.

Right. Like why is bash useful? Why is the file system useful? Why not just use tools? Yeah. Agents — I mean, you can also make a proposal. I'll talk about that a bit later. The agents build their own context. Thinking about code generation for non-coding: like we use code gen to generate docs, query the web, like do data analysis, take unstructured actions.

So there's a lot of like, this can be pretty counterintuitive to some people. And again, in the prototyping session, we'll go over how to use code generation for non-coding agents.

And yeah, every agent has a container or is hosted locally because this is Cloud Code. It needs a file system, it needs bash, it needs to be able to operate on it. And so it's a very, very different architecture. I'm not planning to talk too much about the architecture today, but we can at the end if that's what people are interested in. Or sorry, by architecture, I mean hosting architecture.

How do you host an agent and what are best practices there? We can talk about that at the end.

yeah, so well, let me pause there because I feel like I covered a lot already. Any questions so far on the agent SDK, agents, yeah? Like what you get from it? Can you explain what "code generation for non-coding" means exactly? Yeah, this is—

like basically, when you ask for code to do a task, right, like let's say that you ask it to find the weather in San Francisco and, you know, tell me what I should wear or something, right? What it might do is it might start

writing a script to fetch a weather API, right, and then start, like, maybe it wants it to be reusable. Maybe you want to do this pretty often, right? So it might fetch the weather API and then, like, maybe even get your location dynamically based on your IP address and then it will, like, you

know, check the weather and then maybe like call out to a sub-agent to give you recommendations. Maybe there's an API for your closet or wardrobe, right? It's like, so that's an example.

I think that, like, it's kind of for any single example. We can talk over how you might use code gen. A lot of it is like composing APIs is like the high-level way to—yeah, yeah, workflow versus agent. For repetitive tasks or, you know, like a business process that is always the same, do you still prefer an agent versus a fully deterministic workflow?

So we do have—oh sure, yeah—so the question was about workflows versus agents and would you still use a cloud agent SDK for workflows, right? Yes. And so I mean, we just sort of tell you what we do internally here basically. What we do internally is we've done a lot of GitHub automations and Slack automations built on the cloud agent SDK.

We have a bot that triages issues when they come in. That's a pretty workflow-like thing, but we've still found that in order to triage issues we want it to be able to clone the codebase and sometimes spin up a Docker container and test it and things like that.

So it still ends up being like a lot of steps in the middle that need to be quite free-flowing, and then you give structured output at the end. So yes.

All right, I'll take one more question. Yeah, so could you talk about security and guardians—like, if, you know, we're using cloud agent SDK and you lean towards using bash as the, you know, all-powerful generic tool, is the onus on building the agent builder to make sure that you're preventing against common attack vectors, or is that something the model is doing?

Yeah, so I think this is sort of like the Swiss cheese—oh yes. Okay, so the question was permissions on the bash tool, right, or like how do you think about permissions and guardrails, and like when you're giving the agent this much power over your environment or the computer how do you make sure it's aligned, right?

The way we think about this is what we call the Swiss cheese defense. Like, there is on every layer some defenses and together we hope that it blocks everything. So obviously on the model layer we do a lot of alignment there.

We actually just put out a really good paper on reward hacking—super recommend you check that out—so definitely I think cloud models like we try and make them very, very aligned.

Then there is the harness itself, right. We have a lot of permissioning and prompting and we do an ST path parser on the bash tool, for example, so we know fairly reliably what the bash tool is actually doing—definitely not something you want to build yourself.

And finally the last layer is sandboxing. So let's say that someone has maliciously taken over your agent: what can it actually do? We've included a sandbox where you can sandbox network requests and sandbox file system operations outside of the final system.

Ultimately that's what they call the lethal trifecta—like the ability to execute code in an environment, change the file system, and exfiltrate the code. I think I'm getting the leaf trifecta a little bit wrong there, but the idea is basically if they can exfiltrate your information back out, that's bad. So sandboxing the network is a good way of doing it.

If you're hosting on a sandbox container—Cloudflare, Modal, or, you know, Daytona—these sandbox providers have also done some level of security. It's like you're not posting it on your personal computer or on a computer with your prod secrets or something. So yeah, lots of different layers, and we can talk more about hosting in depth.

Okay, so I'm gonna talk a little bit about bash and "all you need." I think this is something that—oh yeah—this is like my shtick. I'm just gonna keep talking about this until everyone agrees with me or like I think there's something that we found adentropic. I think it's sort of something I discovered once I got here: bash is what makes code so good.

I think you guys have probably seen code mode or programmatic changes, right, like the different ways of composing MSTP's—Cloudflare put out some blog posts on that, we put out some blog posts. The way I think about code mode, or bash, is that it was like the first code mode, right?

So the bash tool allows you to store the results of your tool calls to files, store memory, dynamically generate scripts and call them, compose functionality. Like, tail graph lets you use existing software like FFmpeg or LibreOffice, right? There's a lot of interesting and powerful things the bash tool can do.

Think about, again, what made cloud code so good. If you were designing an agent harness, maybe what you would do is you'd have a search tool and a lint tool and an execute tool, right? Like, every time you thought of a new use case you need another tool. Now cloud just uses graph, right? Those are your package manager, so it runs like npm run test. ts or index. ts or whatever.

Lint, right, and you can find out how you lint. If you don't have a linter, it can be like what if I install ESLint for you, right?

This is like the first programmatic tool calling, first code mode. You can do a lot of different actions very generically. To talk about this in the context of non-coding agents: let's say we have an email agent and the user is like, "Okay, how much did I spend on ride-sharing this week?" It's got one tool call, or generally the ability to search your inbox.

So it can run a query like, "Hey, search Uber or Lyft," and without bash it searches Uber or Lyft, it gets like a hundred emails or something, and now it's just got to like think about it. I mean, a good analogy is imagine if someone came to you with a stack of papers and said, "Hey, how much did I spend on ride-sharing this week? " You'd read through my emails; that would be hard.

You need very good precision and recall.

With bash, though, let's say there's a Gmail search script. It takes a query function and then you can start to save that query function to a file or type it, you can grab four prices, you can then add them together, you can check your work too.

You can say, "Okay, let me grab all my prices, store those in a file with line numbers, and then let me be able to check afterwards: was this actually a price? What does each one correlate to? " There's a lot more dynamic information you can do to check your work with the bash tool.

This is just a simple example, but hopefully it shows the power of the composability of bash. I'll pause there—any questions on "bash is all you need," the bash tool, anything I can make a little bit clearer?

That's on the yellow mode—we probably do, I mean internally we don't, but that's just—I think we just have a higher security posture. Yeah, I'm not sure; I can probably pull that. Any other questions on bash? Okay cool.

Just to give you some more examples: like let's say you had an email API and you wanted to go through and tell me who emailed me this week, right? So you've got two APIs: an inbox API and a contact API. This is a way you can do it via bash. You can also do it via CodeGen. This is kind of like enough Bash that it is CodeGen—Bash is ostensibly a CodeGen tool.

And then, let's say you had a video meeting agent and you wanted to find all the moments where the speaker said "quarterly results" in this earnings call. You can use FFmpeg to slice up the video, you can use JQ to start analyzing the information afterwards. So yeah, lots of powerful ways to use Bash.

So I'm going to talk a little bit about workflows and agents. You can do both. You could build workflows and agents on the agent SDK. Yeah, agents are like Cloud Code. If you are building something where you want to talk to it in natural language and take action flexibly, that's where you're building an agent.

You have an agent that talks to your business data and you want to get insights, dashboards, answer questions, write code, or something. That's an agent.

A workflow is kind of like we do a lot of GitHub Actions, for example. You define the inputs and outputs very closely, so you're like, "OK, take in a PR and give me a code review." Both of these you can use the agent SDK for. When building workflows, you can use structured outputs—we just released this; you can Google "agent SDK structured outputs." But yeah, you can do both.

I'm going to primarily be talking about agents right now; a lot of the things you can learn from this are applicable to workflows as well. So yeah, we'll talk about this. Wait, show of hands: how many people have designed an agent loop before? OK, cool.

I mean, the number one meta learning for designing an agent loop, to me, is just to read the transcripts over and over again. Every time you see the agent run, just read it and figure out, "Hey, what is it doing? Why is it doing this? Can I help it out somehow?" We'll do some of that later when we build an agent loop.

But here are the three parts to an agent loop: first, gather context; second, take action; and third, verify the work. This is not the only way to build an agent loop, but it's a pretty good way to think about it.

Gathering context for Cloud Code is grabbing and finding the files needed. For an email agent, it's finding the relevant emails. Think about how it finds its context—that's very important and often underthought.

Then taking action: how does it do its work? Does it have the right tools, like code generation or Bash—these are more flexible ways of taking action. Then verification is another really important step. If you're thinking about building an agent, think about: can you verify its work? If you can verify its work, it's a great candidate for an agent.

If you can't verify its work, like coding, you can verify by linting and at least make sure it compiles, so that's great.

If you're doing, let's say, deep research, for example, it's actually a lot harder to verify your work. One way you can do it is by citing sources. So that's like a step in verification. But obviously, research is less verifiable than code in some ways, because code has a compile step. You can also execute it and see what it does.

So I think thinking on, as we build agents, the ones that are closest to being very general are the ones with the verification step that is very strong.

So I think there was a question here. Yeah. So when do you generate a plan of the work that you do when you run through it? Yeah, I mean, you might. Did you ask the question? Oh, yeah. Sorry. The question was, when do you generate a plan before you run through it? So in Cloud Code, you don't always generate a plan.

But if you want to, you'd insert it between the gathering context and taking action step. And so plans help the agent think through step by step, but they add some latency. And so there is some trade-off there. But yeah, the agent SDK helps you do some planning as well.

Can you make the agent create that to-do list? Or like 100% sure that it will create that to-do list and run by it? Yeah. So the question was, will the agent create the to-do list? Yes. If you're using the agent SDK, we have some to-do tools that come with it. And so it will maintain and check off to-dos. And you can display them as you go.

Any other questions about this right now? OK, cool. So I'm going to quickly talk about how do you do this stuff? What are your tools for doing it? There are three things you can do. You have tools, bash, and code generation. And traditionally, I think a lot of people are only thinking about tools. And basically, one of the call to action is just figuring out thinking about it more broadly.

Tools are extremely structured and very, very reliable. If you want to have as fast an output as possible with minimal errors, minimal retries, tools are great. Cons: there is high context usage. If anyone's built an agent with 50 or 100 tools, they take up a lot of context. And the model gets a little bit confused. There's no discoverability of the tools. And they're not composable.

And I say tools in the sense of if you're using a messages or completion API right now, that's how the tools work. Of course, there's code mode and programmatic tool calling, so you can sort of blend some of these.

Then there's bash. So bash is very composable, like static scripts, low context usage. It can take a little bit more discovery time. Because let's say that you have whatever. You have the Playwright CLI, the Playwright bash tool. You can do playwright --help to figure out all the things you can do. But the agent needs to do that every time.

So it needs to discover what it can do, which is kind of powerful. That helps take away some of the high context usage but adds some latency. There might be slightly lower call rates, just because it has a little bit more time to — it needs to find the tools and what it can do. But this will definitely improve as it goes.

And then finally, CodeGen. Highly composable, dynamic scripts. They take the longest to execute. So they need linting, possibly compilation. API design becomes a very, very interesting step here. And I'll talk more about how to think about API design in an agent. But yeah, I think this is the three tools you have.

So yeah, using tools, you still want some tools. But you want to think about them as atomic actions. Your agent usually needs to execute in sequence. And you need a lot of control over it. For example, in Cloud Code, we don't use bash to write a file. We have a write-file tool, because we want the user to be able to see the output and approve it.

And we're not really composing write-file with other things. It's a very atomic action. Sending an email is another example. Any sort of destructible or irreversible change is definitely a tool that's a good place for that.

Then you've got bash. So for example, there are composable actions like searching a folder using GitHub, linting code and checking for errors, or memory. And so yeah, you can write files to memory. And that can be your bash. Bash can be your memory system, for example.

And then finally, you've got code generation. So if you're trying to do this highly dynamic, very flexible logic, composing APIs, like you're doing data analysis, or deep research, or reusing patterns. And so yeah, we'll talk more about code generation in a bit.

Any questions so far about the SDK loop, or tools versus bash versus code gen? Yeah? Yeah. I was going to ask, how are you going to have any ready-made tools for offloading tool file results. Offloading tool file results, like into the file system? Or? Let's say it goes into bash, and then context explodes. Is it like, type your command, then do everything up? OK.

Or otherwise, just long outputs polluting your history? Sure, yeah, yeah, yeah. How about all the time just offloading them to files?

Yeah, yeah. I think that's a good common practice. I think we — I remember seeing some PRs about this very recently on Cloud Code, about handling very long outputs. I don't know exactly. I think we are moving towards a place where more and more things are being just stored in the file system. And this is a good example. Yeah, storing long outputs over time.

I think generally, prompting the agent to do this is a good way to think about it. Or even if you have, I think something I just do always now is whenever I have a tool call, I save the results of the tool call to the file system so that you can search across it and then have the tool call return the path of the result. Just because that helps it recheck its work.

So yes? Do you find that you need to use the skills constructor to help plot along to use Bash better or out of the box? I know that's not necessarily. Yeah, so the question was about skills and do we need skills to use Bash better. Yeah, for context skills, maybe I could learn to skills.

OK, yeah. Skills are basically a way of allowing our agent to take longer complex tasks and load in things via context. For example, we have a bunch of docx skills. And these docx skills tell it how to do code generation to generate these files. And so I think overall, skills are basically just a collection of files.

They're also an example of being very file-system- or Bash-tool-filled because they're really just folders that your agent can cd into and read.

And so what we found the skills are really good for is pretty repeatable instructions that need a lot of expertise in them. For example, we released a front-end design skill recently that I really, really like. And it's really just a very detailed and good prompt on how to do front-end design. But it comes from our best AI front-end engineer. You know what I mean?

And he really put a lot of thought and iteration to it. So that's one way of using skills.

Quick question. Yes. Why use that front-end skill? Sure. It's pretty powerful. Thanks for what you said. I want to understand, there are multiple .md files. .md is also there. And it is also at the user level. And then there are skill.md files. Is there a priority order? Should some stuff be relegated to plot.md and some other stuff should be coming to skill.md?

So the question was about skill. md versus plot. md and how to think about that. And I think I will say all of these concepts are so new. Even plot code is released eight or nine months ago. Skills were released two weeks ago. I won't pretend to know all of the best practices for everything. I think generally skills are a form of progressive context disclosure.

And that's a pattern that we've talked about a bunch with Bash and preferring that over purely normal tool calls.

It's a way of the agent being like, OK, I need to do this. Let me find out how to do this. And then let me read in the skill. md. So you ask it to make a docx file. And then it cds into the directory, reads how to do it, writes some scripts, and keeps going. So yeah, I think there's still some intuition to build around what exactly you define as a skill and how you split it out.

But yeah, I think lots of best practices to learn there still.

So yesterday, you talked about the future of skills. Do you see skills as ultimately becoming part of the model? And do you think that some of the skills are just a way to bridge the gap for now? Yeah, so the question was, are skills ultimately part of the model? Are they a way to bridge the gap? I missed Barry and Nash's talk yesterday.

But yeah, I think roughly the idea is that the model will get better and better doing a wide variety of tasks. And skills are the best way to give it out-of-distribution tasks.

But I would broadly say that it's really, really hard, especially if you're not at a lab to tell where the models are going exactly. My general rule of thumb is I try and rethink or rewrite my agent code every six months just because I'm like, things have probably changed enough that I've baked in some assumptions here.

And so I think our agent SDK is built to as much as possible advance with capabilities. The Bash tool will get better and better. We're building it on top of Cloud Code. So as Cloud Code evolves, you'll get those wins out of the gate.

But at the same time, things are so different now than they were a year ago in terms of AI engineering. And I think a general best practice to me is sort of like, hey, we can write code 10 times faster. We should throw out code 10 times faster as well. And I think thinking about not so hedging your bets on where is the future right now, but what can we do today that really works?

And let's get market share today and not be afraid to throw out code later.

If you're a startup, this is arguably your largest advantage that you have over competitors. Larger companies have six-month incubation cycles. And so they're always stuck in the past of the agent capabilities. And so your advantage is that you can be like, hey, the capabilities are here right now. Let me build something that uses this right now.

So yeah. Any other questions on? We're talking about skills in Bash. OK, it seems like there are a lot of skill questions. Yeah, I think at the back, someone might have to shout. Yeah, so why would you use a skill versus an API? They look very similar to the Python program there. It could be a package, right?

Yeah, so the question was, why use a skill versus an API? Good question. I think that these are all forms of progressive disclosure, basically, to the agent to figure out what it needs to do. And I'll go over examples of, you just have an API in our prototyping session. It's totally use-case dependent, right? I don't think there's a general rule.

I think it's like, read the transcript and see what your agent wants. If your agent always thinks about the API better as like an api. ts file or something, or api. py file, do that. That's great.

I think skills are sort of an introduction into thinking about the file system as a way of storing context. And they're a great abstraction. But there are many ways to use the file system. And I should say that something about skills is that you need the Bash tool. You need a virtual file system, things like that.

So the agent SDK is basically the only way to really use skills to their full extent right now.

Back there? Can we expect a marketplace for skills? Yeah, the question was, can we expect a marketplace for skills? So yeah, Cloud Code has a plug-in marketplace that you can also use with the agent SDK. We're evolving that over time. It was very much a V0. And by marketplace, I'm not sure if people will be charging for this exactly. It's more just like a discovery system, I think.

But yeah, that exists right now. You can do /plug-ins in Cloud Code. And you can find some.

What's your current thinking about when you're going to reach for the SDK to solve a problem? Yes, the question is, when do I use the SDK to solve a problem? If I'm building an agent, basically, I think that my overall belief is that for any agent, the Bash tool gives you so much power and flexibility.

And using the file system gives you so much power and flexibility that you can always eat into performance gains over it. And so in the prototyping part of this talk, we're going to look at an example with only tools and an example with Bash and the file system and compare those two. And yeah, that's what I mean by being Bash-fulfilled.

I just start from the agent SDK. And I think a lot of people out in the topic have started doing that as well. Of course, I do want to say that there are lots of times where the agent SDK is kind of annoying because you've got this network sandbox container, and you're like, I don't want to do this. I want to run on my browser locally. I totally get that.

There is a real performance trade-off. The way I think about it is React versus jQuery. When I was coming up, I was very into web dev. And I was using jQuery and Backbone. And then React came out. And it was by Facebook. Here's JSX. We just made this up. And now there's a bundler. And I'm like, it's so annoying.

But it generally makes the model, or it made web apps more powerful. And I think we're a sort of like, the HMSD is sort of like the reactive HM frameworks to me. Because it's like, we build our own stuff on top of it so you know it's real. And all the annoying parts of it are just like, things where we're annoyed about it too, but we're like, it just works.

Like, you got to do this. So yeah. Yeah, OK, more skill questions, I guess? Yeah, right here. One of the questions is that. Oh, sure, the Bash question. Great, I love Bash. If you have custom internal, like, Bash tools, how do you let the agent discover that? Or do those have to become a tool?

OK, the question is, if you have custom agent Bash tools, how do you let the agent discover that? By custom Bash tools, do you mean like, Bash scripts? Yeah, Bash scripts, yeah. Yeah. Yeah, so I think. You just put it in the file system. And you tell it, like, hey, here's a script. You can call it.

I'm generally thinking in the context of the Cloud Agent SDK where it has the file system and the Bash tools are tied together. This is kind of an anti-pattern I see sometimes where people are like, oh, we're going to host the Bash tool in this virtualized place. And it's not going to interact with other parts of the agent loop, you know?

And that sort of makes it hard. Because if you've got a tool result that's saving a file, then your Bash tool can't read it, unless it's all in one container. So does that answer your question? Yeah, kind of. I mean, so you're saying you just put it in system prompt or something? Yeah, just put it in system prompt and be like, hey, you have access to this.

I would sort of design all my CLI scripts to have a --help or something so that the model can call that. And then it can progressively disclose every subcommand inside of the script, yeah. Yeah, there? Yeah. So my question is around when to reach for the agent SDK.

So have you designed, or rather, would you recommend someone use the agent SDK to build a generic chat agent as compared to, like, oh, I'm building an agent where you have some input, and the agent goes and does some stuff.

And finally, I care about the output, as compared to, let's say, someone like, are you using or do you foresee using the agent to build, like, the agent SDK to build, like, Cloud. ai, which is a more traditional chatbot, than Cloud Code? Yeah. So the question is, when do we reach for the agent SDK? Does, like, would we use the agent SDK to build Cloud.

ai, which is a more traditional chatbot than Cloud Code?

One, I think Cloud Code is, like, a very, like, interface is not a traditional chatbot interface, but, like, the inputs and outputs are, right? Like, you input code in, you get, like, or you input text in, you get text out, and you can take actions along the way. You might have seen that, like, when we rolled out doc creation for Cloud.

ai, now it has the ability to spin up a file system and, like, create spreadsheets and PowerPoint files and things like that by generating code.

And so that is, like, you know, we're in the midst of sort of, like, merging our agent loops and stuff like that. But broadly, like, Cloud.ai is getting more and more, like, you see it with skills and the memory tool and stuff, more and more file system builds, right? So we do think it's, like, a broad thing that you can use just generally. And happy to talk through examples.

Yeah, one more question, and then we'll keep going. I'm still trying to understand the rule of thumb on when to build a tool or use a tool, when to wrap something with a script, or just let the agent go wild on the batch. I'll give you an example. Let's say I need to access a database from time to time. I can use an MCP. I can wrap it in a script.

And I can just let the agent call an endpoint from that, directly from batch, right?

Yeah, great question, great question. So I'm still trying to grok, like, when to use tools versus batch versus Cogen. And you gave an example, like, OK, I have a database. I want the agent to be able to access it in some way. What should I do? Should I create a tool that queries the database in some way? Should I use the batch? Should I use Cogen, right?

These are all, these are three ways of doing it. I think that they are, like, you could use any of them. And I think, like, part of it is, like, I think, unfortunately, there's no, like, single best practice, right? This is, like, kind of a system design problem. But let's say that you want to access your database via a tool. You would do that if your database was very, very structured.

And you have to be very careful about, like, I don't know, you're accessing, like, user-sensitive information or something like that. And you're like, hey, I can only take in this input. And I need to, like, give this output. And I have to mask everything else about the database from the agent, right? Obviously, that, like, sort of limits what the agent can do, right?

Like, it can't write a very dynamic query, right? If you're writing a full-on SQL query, I would definitely use Bash or Cogen, just because when the model's writing a SQL query, it can make mistakes. And the way it fixes its mistakes is by, like, linting or, like, running the file, looking at the output, seeing if there are errors, and then iterating on it, right?

And so I generally, like, if I'm building an agent today, I'm giving it as much access to my database as possible. And then I'm, like, putting in guardrails, right? Like, I'm probably limiting its, like, write access in different ways. But probably what I would do is, like, I would give it write access and put in specific rules and then give it feedback if it tries to do something it can't do.

You know what I mean?

And so I know this is, like, kind of a hard problem. I think this is the, like, set of problems for us to solve, right? Like, we built a Bash tool parser. And that's a super annoying problem. But we need to solve that in order to, like, let the agent work generally, right? And same thing with, like, databases. Like, yes, it's quite hard to understand what is a query doing.

But if you can solve that, you can let your agent work more generally over time.

So yeah, I think thinking about it, like, flexibly as much as possible and keeping tools to be, like, very, very, like, sort of atomic actions, right? That you need a lot of guarantees around. A follow-up to the same thing. Yeah. How do you ensure that all these access controls are taken care of? How do you ensure?

So the question is, how do you ensure that the role-based access controls are taken care of? Usually, that's in, like, how you provision your API key or your back-end service or something like that, right? Right. I think that, like, probably what I do is, like, I create, like, temporary API keys.

Sometimes people create proxies in between to insert the API keys if you're concerned about exfiltration of that.

But yeah, I would create, like, API keys for your agents that are scoped in certain ways. And so then on the back-end, you can sort of check it's, like, what it's trying to do. And, like, if it's an agent, you can, like, give it different feedback. So yeah.

All right, I have one more question. Anything you could tell us more about the memory tool, the internal memory tool? I'm not trying to, like, keep a secret. I don't know exactly. Like, I haven't read the code. But I think it generally works on the file system. And so it's supposed to be a Cloud Agent SDK, or is it already a bit of a thing?

I would say that, like, we've had this question a bunch. I would just use the file system in the Cloud Agent SDK. I would just create, like, a memories folder or something and tell it to write memories there. It's like, I don't know the exact implementation of the memory tool. But it does use the file system in that way. So yeah.

All right, yeah, last question on this, yeah. How do you manage, for the best in the code, how you are managing the, like, reusability? Suppose the same agent is allowed to hundreds of users, and same code every time it is generating and every time it is executing. So how can we use the reusability?

Yeah, that's a really good question. So yeah, let's say you have two agents interacting with two different people. The question is, like, how do you think about reusability between agents, or how do agents communicate, right? I think this is a thing to be discovered.

I think there's a lot of best practices and system design to be done on, like, because traditionally with web apps, you're serving one app to a million people, right?

And with agents, like with Cloud Code, we serve a one-to-one container. When you use Cloud Code on the web, it's your container, right? And so there's not a lot of communication between containers. It's a very, very different paradigm. I'm not going to say that I know exactly the best system design to do that, right?

And I think there's lots of best practices on, like, OK, these agents are reusing work. How can we give them, like, general scripts that combine together the work that they've done? How can we make them share it? I would generally think this is sort of like a tangent on, like, agent communication frameworks.

I would say that we probably don't need, like, a whole. I think there's more of a personal opinion. I think, like, we probably don't need to reinvent, like, a new communication system. The agents are good at using the things that we have HTTP requests, and bash tools, and API keys, and named pipes, and all of these things.

And so probably the agents are just making HTTP requests back and forth from each other using HTTP server. There's a bunch of interesting work there. I've seen people make a virtual forum for their agents to communicate. And they post topics, and reply, and stuff like that. Kind of cool. I think there's a lot of things to explore and discover there.

And so, like, if you have a data source, if you can convert it into a SQL query, then your agent really knows how to search SQL, right? So thinking about this transformation step is really, really interesting. It's a great way of, like, designing, like, an agentic search interface.

All right. So this is a syntax, for example, that the agent's pretty familiar with, like, B3 to B5, right? And so you can design an agentic search interface, which is like this, right, like, B3, B5, or something, right?

So, like, your agentic search interface can take in a range, right, can take in a range string, right? And these are things that, like, the agent knows pretty well, right? Like you can do SQL queries, right? The agent knows SQL queries pretty well, right?

And like these, you can also do XML, right? Sorry, the font is so small. Okay. Yeah, you can also do XML. I'm not sure if you guys know, but like XLSX files are XML in the back end, right? And XML is very structured.

You can do like an XML search query and there are different libraries that can do that. So that's one example, right? Is like how do you search and gather context? And I hope this sort of like illustrates to you that like gather context is really, really creative, right?

And like there's so many iterations and if you've just, if you've only tried one iteration, it's probably not enough, right? Like think about like as many different ways as you can, like try these out, right?

Like try SQL, try the sort of, try the grep and awk and like all of these things and have a few tests that you're trying across different things and see what the agent likes and what it doesn't like.

It's going to be different for each case. Yeah. Yeah, because the question is like where does the knowledge come from? Is it the model? Is it like what do I mean by agent? Yeah, generally, I think what you're looking for is like you have a problem. You want to make it as a distribution as possible for the agent, right?

And so the agent knows a lot about a lot of different things. It knows a lot about, for example, finance, right? So if you ask it to make a DCF model, it knows what DCF is, right? And you can, if you want to give it more information, you can make a skill, right?

But so it knows what DCF is, it knows what SQL is, can it combine those things together, right? And so like, ideally, you want to like, your problem is going to be out of distribution in some way, right?

Like, there's some like information that's not on the internet or something that you have, or something somewhat unique to you, and you want to try and like massage it to be as in distribution as possible.

And, yes, it's very, very creative, I think, like, you know, it's not like a, it's not a science to me, but it's very much like an art. So yeah, okay, so we've tried gathering context, then taking action,

we can probably do a lot of the same stuff here that we've done before, right? Like we can do like, insert to the array, right? If we've got like a SQL interface, right, we can, we can do SQL query, we can edit XML. These are like, often very similar, right?

Like taking action and gathering context that you probably want a similar API back and forth. And then the last thing is verifying work, right? Like, how do you think about, how do you think about that? Check for null pointers, right, is one of the ways to do it. Any other ideas on verification? Or?

Yeah. Sorry, I'm a bit confused. Oh, yeah. Yeah. Like, when, when you're using other SDKs to build agents, I don't need to tell it how it should gather the context. Sure. I just give it the context and explain this is what's like, basically explain in plain English. Yeah, what is meant to do?

Yeah. And what I tend to do, and you tell me if I'm wrong, I actually end up creating a separate agent for QA. Oh, interesting. To verify because I don't trust the agent to verify itself. But I'm just, I'm just a bit confused about the level of detail I need to provide the agent in that example.

Yeah, okay. So the question is about giving context to the agent versus having it gather its own context. You mentioned that you sometimes use a Q&A agent. Can I ask like, what domain you're building your agent in? Cybersecurity. Okay, sure. Yeah.

Um, I think that I think I need to like look into more specifics, but the cloud agent SDK is great for cybersecurity. And like, I would generally push people on like, let the agent gather context as much as possible. You know, like, let it find its own work as much as possible. You're trying to give it the tools to find its own work.

The way I think about this is kind of like, let's say they someone locked you in a room. And they were they were like giving you tasks, you know, like, so that's what your what your job was like a Mr. Beast sort of like scenario, right? Like you get $500,000 if you stay in this room for six months.

Then like, like someone's giving you a message, what tools would you want to be able to do it? Right? Like, would you just want like a list of papers? Or, like, would you want a calculator or like a computer, right? Probably I would want a computer, right? I'd want Google, I'd want like all of these things. Right.

And so like, I wouldn't want the person to send me like a stack of papers, be like, hey, this is probably all the information you need. I'd rather just be like, hey, just give me a computer, give me the problem. Let me search it and figure it out. Right. And so that's how I think about agents as well. Like they need like, like, you know, they're stuck in a room, I need to give them tools.

So if you can go back to the slides, you have to the graph, you have to the graph, like this, you know, or so basically, that gathering context is basically these are the two words I'm offering. Yeah, exactly. Yeah, you're giving it like, maybe an API for code generation, maybe I'm giving a SQL tool, maybe I'm giving a bash. These are all like examples.

Right. So, yeah. So for all the agents that you're managing in a certain state, do agents share the same context window? Interesting. Yeah.

So do agents share the context window? I think I think this is like an interesting question overall about how you manage context. I think, and I haven't talked about this too much yet. But sub agents are like a very, very important way of managing context.

I think that this is like, we're using more and more sub agents inside of Cloud Code. And I would think about, like, doing sub agents very generally. So like, what we might do for this, the main agent is maybe we have a search sub agent, right?

So like, sub agents are great for when you need to do a lot of work and return an answer to the main agent. So for search, let's say the question is like, how do I find my revenue in 2026? Maybe you need to do a bunch of results.

Maybe you need to, like, search the internet, maybe you need to search a spreadsheet, things like that. And there's a bunch of things that don't need to go into the context of the main agent; the main agent just needs to see the final result. Right. And so that's a great sub agent task.

I don't have a dedicated sub-agent slide here, but like, yeah, they're very, very useful. And I think a great way to think about things. Are you saying yes, you'd use a sub agent here, you'd use a skill? How would you think about this? Yeah, definitely.

So question on like, do you use sub agents? I'm sure it'll work. Oh, sure. Thank you. Appreciate it.

Okay, yeah. Can you use sub agents for verification? Yes, I think this is a pattern. I think like, ideally, the best form of verification is rule-based, right? You're like, is there like a null pointer or something? That's like easy verification.

It doesn't lint or compile—like, as many rules as you can, try and insert them. And again, be creative, right? Like, for example, in Cloud Code, if the agent tries to write to a file that we know it hasn't read yet, like we haven't seen it enter the read cache, we throw it an error.

We tell it, like, hey, you haven't read this file yet. Try reading it first. Right. And that's an example of sort of like a deterministic tool that we insert into the verification step.

And so as much as possible, like anytime you are thinking about verification, first step is like, what can you do deterministically? What outputs can you do? And again, when you're choosing which types of agents to make, the agents that have more deterministic rules are better.

You know, like, they just like, like, it just makes a lot of sense. Right. So, of course, as the models get better and better at reasoning, then you can have these sub agents that check the work of the main agent.

The main thing there is to like avoid context pollution. So you probably wouldn't want to like fork the context; you'd probably want to start a new context session and just be like, hey, yeah, adversarially check the work of this.

Like, this, this output was made by a junior analyst at McKinsey or something. They graduated from like not a great school. Like, you know, like, like just feed in a bunch of stuff and then tell it to critique it. Right. Like, that's like one of the tools of the sub agent.

Right. And so, yeah, the more you like, yeah, as the models get better and better, that sort of verification will become better as well. But doing it deterministically is like a great start. You know, just a question about the verified work.

So, yeah. So, let's say we found null pointers, it's probably easy to just say, okay, fix it. But, like, you know, let's say we deploy to production and the client decides to do it—that's not us—and they somehow get into a spot where the whole spreadsheet is deleted.

And so, like, on what level do we need to bake in, like, the ability to, like, undo rules? Because, like, let's say the QA agent determined that their spreadsheet is empty, not necessarily is able to undo. So, like, what was your advice there?

Yeah, so the question is, like, how do you think about state and, like, undoing and redoing, being able to fix errors, basically, right? I think this is, like, a really good question. And, honestly, another sort of, like, when you think about, like, what are agents good at, right?

Like, or what problem domains are agents good at? How reversible is the work is, like, a really good intuition, right? So, code is quite reversible. You can just, like, go back, you can undo the git history.

We come with, like, you know, these atomic operations right out of the gate, right? Like, I use git constantly through Cloud Code. So that's, like, a really good example.

A really bad example is computer use, you know, because computer use is not reversible in state, right? Like, let's say you go to, like, DoorDash.com and you add, like, the user wants you to order a Coke and you order a Pepsi.

Now, like, you can't just go back and click on the Coke. You have to, like, go to the cart and you have to remove the Pepsi, right? And so your mistake has, like, compounded this, like, you know, this state and the state machine has gotten more complex, right?

And so, like, whenever we're dealing with, like, very, very complex state machines that you can't undo or redo, it does become harder, right? And I think one of the questions for you as an engineer is, like, can you turn this into a reversible state machine, kind of like you said?

Can you store state between checkpoints such that the user can be like, oh, my spreadsheet is messed up right now. Just go back to the previous checkpoint, right? Potentially even can the model go back to previous checkpoints.

I think someone had this, like, time travel tool that they were giving one of the coding agents, which was kind of cool, where you're, like, it's, like, you can time travel back to a point before this happened. You know what I mean? It's kind of fun.

I think, like, all of these tools, some of them don't work that well yet, but, you know, we'll get there. Yeah, thinking about state and verification is very useful, right? So, yeah. Quick question in the back. Yeah.

I'm kind of curious about scale. So what if the spreadsheet is, like, millions of rows, billions, hundreds of thousands of columns, right? Or just, like, any sort of database. Like, in that type of situation, how would you go about searching? There's obviously a context limit. You have to cognize more. Yeah, this is great.

I probably should have done the spreadsheet example as my coding example. For a preview, my coding, like, agent is a Pokemon agent. Probably a spreadsheet would have been better. Okay. The question was: what if the spreadsheet is very big? If you have a million rows, how do you think about a hundred columns, yeah, a hundred thousand columns or a hundred columns or whatever?

Like, how do you think about it, right? Like, your database is also very big. Like, how do you do that?

I think for all of these things, one, of course, as the data becomes larger and larger, it's just a harder problem. Like, you know, it just absolutely is. Your accuracy will go down, right? Like, Cloud Code is worse in larger codebases than it is in smaller codebases, right?

As the models get better, they will get better at all of that. For all of these, I would think about, like, how would I do this? If I had a spreadsheet that was, like, a million rows and a million columns, what would I do?

I mean, I would need to start searching for it, right? I would need to be, like, if I'm searching for revenue, I'd be, like, searching Control‑F "Revenue", and then I'd go check each of these, like, results, and I'd be, like, is this right? And then, like, I'd see, like, hey, is there a number here?

And then I'd probably keep a scratchpad, like a new sheet where I'm, like, hey, like, equals revenue equals this, you know, and store this reference and keep going. So I think that's a good way of thinking about it, is, like, the model should, you should never, like, read the entire spreadsheet into context because it would take too much, right?

Like, you want to give it, like, the starting amount of context, and that's also how you work, right? Like, let's say that you open up the spreadsheet. What you see is rows is this, right? You see, like, the first 10 rows and the first, like, you know, 30 columns or something, right? That's what you see. You don't load all of it into context right away.

You probably have an intuition for, like, hey, I should load more of this into context, right? And, like, oh, I should navigate to this other sheet, right? And this other sheet has more data, right? But you need to, like, sort of gather context yourself, right?

And so the agent can operate in the same way. It can, like, navigate to these sheets, read them, like, try and, like, keep a scratchpad, keep some notes, and keep going. So that's how I would think about it.

Yeah, in the back. Yeah, so my question is about managing context pollution. It actually, I guess, relates to the previous question. Do you have a rule of thumb for, you know, what fraction of the context window to use before you start hitting the mainstream returns, or does it become less effective?

Yeah, the question is, yeah, context management. Do you have a rule of thumb for, like, how much of the context window to use before it becomes less effective? This is actually, I'd say, a pretty interesting problem right now.

I think a lot of times when I talk to people who are using Cloud Code, they're like, I'm on my fifth compact. I'm like, what? Like, I almost have never done a compact before. You know what I mean? Like, I have to, like, test the UX myself by, like, forcing myself to get compacted.

Just because, like, I tend to, like, clear the context window very often, right, when I'm using Cloud Code myself, just because, like, at least in code, the state is in the files of the code base, right?

So let's say that I made some changes. Cloud Code can just look at my Git diff and be like, oh, hey, these are the changes you've made. It doesn't need to know, like, my entire chat history with it, you know, in order to continue a new task, right?

And so in Cloud Code, I clear the context very, very often. And I'm like, hey, look at my outstanding Git changes. I'm working on this. Can you help me extend it in this way, right? That's, like, a way of thinking about it.

And when you're building your own agent, like, let's say we're building a spreadsheet agent, it gets a little bit more complex because your users are less technical, right? And they don't know what a context window is, right? That is, like, I'd say a hard problem. I think there's, like, some UX design there.

Like, can you reset the conversation state, right? Like, can you maybe every time the user asks a new question, can you do your own compact or something? And can you, like, summarize the context?

Does it, like, in a spreadsheet, a lot of the state is in the spreadsheet itself. So it probably doesn't need, you know, to know the entire context. Can you store user preferences as it goes so that you remember some of this stuff? Like, there's a lot of, like, again, like, it's an art.

There's, like, so many different angles and ways in which you can do this, right? But, yeah, you are trying to, like, sort of minimize context usage. You probably don't need some million context or something, you know what I mean? Like, you just need good context management, like UX design. Yeah.

Yeah. I just wanted to ask. The sub-agents were made to protect the conduct of the core agent, right? That's right. Yeah. Sub-agents were made to... Would we be able to use multiple sub-agents and try to make a process where we chunk up the spreadsheet in the case where it's super large so that the agents can kind of run through each portion, like, in parallel with each other? Yeah.

Yeah. I mean, yeah. So, like, one of the things I love about Cloud Code is that we are, like, the best experience for using sub-agents. Like, especially sub-agents with Bash. It is very, very good. I didn't quite realize all the pain.

I think if anyone's going to QCon, I believe Adam Wolf is giving a talk on QCon about how we did the Bash tool. Adam's a legend, and the Bash tool is such a good job. When you're running parallel sub-agents at the same time, Bash becomes, like, very complex, and there are lots of race conditions and stuff like that.

There's a lot of work that we've solved there, right? One of the things I love about Cloud Code is that you can just be like, hey, like, spin up three sub-agents to do this task, and it will do that. In the agent SDK as well, you can just ask it to do that.

Number one, sub-agents are a great primitive in the agent SDK, and I haven't seen anyone do it as well. So that's, like, a big reason to use it. Yes, generally you want these sub-agents to preserve context.

Let's say if you have a spreadsheet, you could potentially have multiple read sub-agents going on at the same time, right? Maybe the main agent is like, hey, can this agent read and summarize sheet one? Can this agent read and summarize sheet two? Can this agent summarize sheet three? Then they return their results, and then the agent maybe spins up more sub-agents again.

We've talked so much about all these different creative ways that you can do things. This is the level at which you should think about it. You should have to think about your problem. You should not, in my opinion, think about how do I spin off a process to make a sub-agent, or the system engineering between, like... what is a compact or something, right?

We take care of all of this for you in the harness so that you can think about, hey, what sub-agents do I need to spin off, right? And how do I create an agentic search interface? And how do I verify its work? These are the really core and hard problems that you have to solve.

Any time you spend not solving these problems and solving lower-level problems, you're probably not delivering value to your users. So yeah, I think sub-agents—big fan of the agent SDK sub-agents, yeah.

Good question. So we have this action and the verification task. So where exactly do we need to put the verification? In this example, let's say after generation of the SQL query, I can verify if the right query is generated or not. That is one part.

Second part is like generating the query and directly executing it. Once I get the output, then I will do the verification. And how can an agent tune dynamically, like, which one is the right part? Yeah, so the question is where do you do verification? Is it only at the end? Do you do it in the middle?

I would say everywhere you can. Just constantly verification, right? Like I said, we do some verification in the read step of Cloud Code. That's a great example. You can do it at the end—you should absolutely do it at the end—but at any other point, if you have rules or heuristics, use them.

For example, one of my rules might be that the total number of columns you should search should be under 10,000 or under 1,000 or something. That's a nice way of doing it. Similarly, maybe you shouldn't be inserting a huge row of values—give feedback to the model and be like, hey, chunk this up. You throw an error and give feedback.

The great thing about the model is it listens to feedback. It will read the error outputs and then it will just keep going. So yeah, verification is definitely a loop. But it's more like verification can happen anywhere and should happen anywhere. Put it in as many places as you can.

All right, I do need to start doing some prototyping, but I'll take one more question. How do we form the steps? How do we say to the agent, go search first, and then do this step, and then do that step? How does the loop actually start—from the start point to the end, how do we define it?

You just tell it. Like, is it in a system prompt, or? Yeah, in a system prompt. With Cloud Code, we just give it the master and we're like, hey, gather context, read your files, do stuff, run your linting. You know what I mean? And so, yeah, with the agent, you don't need to enforce this.

You don't need to tell it you need to do this, because sometimes it might not be necessary. Let's say someone is asking a read-only question for your spreadsheet. You don't need to verify that there are no compiler errors, right, because you haven't done any write operations. So let the agent be intelligent, and in the same way that you would like that same freedom when you're doing your work.

OK, cool. I do want to try and see if I can do some prototyping now that we have this holder as well. OK, yeah—execute, lint—we've done a bunch of Q&A. OK, prototyping. Let's say that you have an agent—you want to build an agent. You come out of this talk and you're like, great, I have a bunch of ideas. How do I do this?

Building an agent should be simple. Your agent at the end should be simple, but simple is not the same as easy. It should be very simple to get started, and it is. Just go to Cloud Code. Give Cloud Code some scripts, libraries, and custom Cloud NPMs, and ask it to do it.

It should be so easy to be like, hey, this is my API. This would be like an API key. Can you go search my customer support tickets or something and organize them by priority, or something like that? Then look at what Cloud Code does and iterate on it.

This is a great way of skipping to the hard domain-specific problems that you have. You have a lot of domain problems—how do you organize your data, your agentic search, how do you create guardrails on your database? These are questions you can start solving right away with Cloud Code.

Try and build something that feels pretty good with Cloud Code. Generally what I've seen is that you can do this and get really good results out of the bat using Cloud Code locally, and you should have high conviction by the end of it.

I think I forgot the "more info" was my AI engineer talk. This is like a deck for internal that we were using. OK, so yeah, I'm going to be inserting this. So yeah, you're getting what we show customers, right? So OK, yeah, use Cloud Code. Again, simple, but simple is not easy.

The amount of code in your agent should not be super large. It doesn't need to be huge or extremely complex. But it does need to be elegant. It needs to be like what the model wants. You want to have this interesting insight: let's turn the model into a SQL query, or let's turn this spreadsheet into a SQL query and then go from there.

So think about it that way. Cloud Code is like a great way of doing that. OK, let's make a Pokemon agent. This is what we're going to do. Pokemon is a game with a lot of information. There are thousands of Pokemon, and you trade a ton of moves. We want to be pretty general.

There is actually a Poke API. The reason I chose Pokemon is because I know that you guys have your own APIs as well, and they're all very unique. I wanted to choose something with a kind of complex API that I haven't tried before.

The Poke API has—you can search up Pokemon like Ditto. You can search up items and things like that. It's got this custom API and you've got everything in the games. One thing your user might want to do is make a Pokemon team. I love Pokemon. I know very little about making an interesting Pokemon team for competitive play. Could my agent help me with that? That'd be cool.

My goal is to make an agent that can chat about Pokemon and then see how far we get. I've done some of this work already and I will open up and show you. The first step in the prompt here is I'm going to do mostly code generation for this.

Is that going to be on GitHub somewhere? Actually, it is. On my personal GitHub, I was going to commit all of this as well. Can I ask a question of you? Yeah. Is it secure GitHub or does it have malware in it? You guys are AI engineers. If you can get owned, that's your fault. So yeah, you can clone this if you'd like. I need to push the last changes up.

OK, so can you guys see this? Should I put it in dark mode instead, or is this fine? Dark mode? Dark hard? OK. How does this work? Can you guys still hear me? OK.

Here's an example: I took the prompt I gave it—hey, go search Poke API for its API and create a TypeScript library. This is all live coded. You can see it's created this interface for Pokemon: get by name, list Pokemon, get all Pokemon, get species and abilities and stuff like that.

It also did it for moves, and then it's created this API that I can use: import Poke API from the Poke API SDK. You can see how it's set this up. Now, this is the Cloud.md. This is a TypeScript SDK for the Poke API.

The modules in the Poke API—here's some key features. I'm asking it to write scripts in the examples directory and then it will execute those scripts to help me with my queries. I give it some example scripts. It doesn't always need all this information, but yeah: fetching Pokemon, listing resources, getting data, things like that.

So this is like my agent, really. It's a prompt I gave it to generate a TypeScript library and then this Cloud.md. I can chat with it in Cloud Code. I'll also show you a version that's just tools.

Here I'm using the messages completion API and I've given it a bunch of tools from the API: getPokemon, getPokemonSpecies, getPokemonAbility, getPokemonType, getMove. You've defined all these tools. You can see I also gave it a prompt and told it to make the tools.

It doesn't want to make 100 tools—there's a ton of PokeAPI data—but there's only so many parameters it can do. So it's got this tool call and now I made a little chat interface with it.

I'm using Bund when I'm prototyping stuff, just because I don't want to compile from TypeScript to JavaScript. Bund has linting built into it; it's a way of simplifying for the agent so the agent doesn't need to remember to compile. TypeScript is better for generation because that's types.

So I'm going to start this Bund chat and then try, OK, what are the generation to Water Pokemon? You'll see that it's starting to search and I'm logging all the tool calls here. This is very, very important because it needs to do the tool calls, and you can see that what it's doing is searching a bunch of Pokemon.

And so you can see that what it's doing is it's searching a bunch of Pokemon.

And then it told me, OK, here are the Water Pokemon for Gen 2, right? It's got Totodile, Croconaw, Feraligatr. You can see how it's thought. Between each step, it's thinking through the previous steps, right?

Now, let's say that I want to do with Cloud Code. I think I might need to delete this example. Oh, yeah. Small question, how do you log the tool calls? Is this just an argument? What you would do, I think, is you would give it access to the internal memory of the ROM. And so let's say that it wanted to find its party. It could search that in memory. So a quick example of using Cloud Code to prototype this. Now, there can be more interesting data here. So I do want to leave time for examples. So I think I'll just sort of like for questions. So I'll just sort of go through an example. Let's say you're making competitive Pokemon. Competitive Pokemon has a lot of different variables and data.

This is like a text file from this online library, basically, which stores all of the Pokemon and their moves and who they work well with and don't work well with and who they're countered by and all of these things. So there's a ton of data here. And it's all in text file, which is actually pretty good for Cloud Code. Because I can say, OK, I'm going to give it a little bit more data.

Normally, I'd put this in the check the data folder. Tell me I want to make a team around Venusaur. Can you give me some suggestions based on this Smogon data? And Smogon is like this online API. And so I'm not entirely sure what it'll do here yet. I haven't done this career before. But we'll see. I think it'll be fun.

But what I wanted to do is sort of graph through this data and sort of figure out from itself, from first principles, not having seen this data before, how can I answer my queries? While it does that, I'll take any questions. Yeah?

First of all, great workshop. So this is like truly on top of Cloud Code. And so my question is, if we weren't simply for this custom interfacing, are we supposed to have Cloud Code running in like a swarm? Or are we somehow able to take Cloud Code far out, just by the agent SDK?

Yeah, so let me show you very quickly what it looks like to use the agent SDK here. So I've already done this file system. And again, I want you to think about the file system as a way of doing context engineering. This is a lot of the inputs into the agent. So my actual agent file is like 50 lines. And it's mostly just random for the page.

I guess, yes, decided to stop it from writing scripts outside of that custom script directory. Again, so leave that uncoded. Yeah, you can see it just runs this query, takes in the working directory, and runs it in a loop. And so probably I'd want to turn into some allowed tools here and stuff. But it's very simple.

And so if I were to productionize this, the first step I'd do is, OK, I've tested it on Cloud Code. It seems to do pretty well. I write this file. Then I put it. There are two ways to do it. So one is, I do think that local apps might be coming back with AI, because I think that there's such an overhead to running it. For example, Cloud Code is a front end app. It works on your computer.

So maybe the way I ship this as a Pokemon app is like, hey, I have an app that you install. And it works locally on your computer. And it's writing scripts. I think that's one way of doing it. The other way is, yeah, you host it in a sandbox. And again, there's a bunch of different sandbox providers that make it really easy.

Like Cloudflare is a good example of using the agent SDK. And it's just like sandbox.start, and then like bun agent.ts. And that's kind of all it takes. They've abstracted away a lot of it. So you run the sandbox, and then you communicate with it. And yeah, I think there is some very interesting stuff that I'm not sure I had time to get to.

But I think some interesting questions are like, how do you do this sort of service? Now we're just spinning up a sandbox per user. There's a lot of, I'd say, best practices to solve here. One thing I just want to call out for you guys to think about, if you're making an agent with a UI, let's say that you have, yeah, my Pokemon agent.

And I wanted to have a UI that is adaptable to the user. Maybe some users are doing team building. Some users are helping with their game. Some users just want pictures of Pokemon. How would I have an agent that adapts in real time to my user? The way I would do it is, in my sandbox, I would have a dev server. And the dev server would expose a port. It would run on bun or node or something.

It would expose a port.

The agent could edit code. And it would live refresh. And your user would be interacting with that website. This is how a lot of site builders, like Lovable and stuff, work. They use sandboxes. And they host, essentially, a dev server. And so thinking about this for your user, if you want a customized interface, this is a great way to do it.

OK, let's see what it did. Let's see. OK, cool. OK, so it's written this script. It's generated, showed me some base stats and suggested a move set and some teammates. And you can see sort of like, let's see, what did it do? Control‑E. Yeah, OK, so you can see here what it started doing. It started searching for Venusaur. And it started finding those types, those Pokemon.

And when it does that, it also gets other Pokemon that mention Venusaur. So it gets its teammates and its counters and stuff.
