Other questions? Yeah. I was curious about how people are monetizing Cloud SDK. They're definitely making their money with it. It's kind of like, if you get the pack on Cloud, you kind of lose the opportunity to get all the cards and sit in the back and put them in. Yeah.

I'm curious how people have been shipping their own Cloud Code SDK to Key, so that they kind of take the montage base of the code that's in Cloud.

Yeah, I do think overall, especially right now, agents are kind of pricey. You know what I mean? Because the models have just started to get agentic. We really focus on having the most intelligent models. Generally, this is just an overall fast business software thing. You'd rather charge fewer people more money than really have a hard problem. And so I think this is still good.

You probably should find these hard use cases. But I would say, number one, make sure you're solving a problem that people want to pay for. It's the number one step. And then number two, yeah, I think you could do subscription or token-based. I think this kind of comes down to how much you expect people to use your product versus how much you expect them to use it occasionally.

Cloud Code, obviously, people use a lot.

And in order to, like, we do a mix of like, if we give you some rate limits, and if you exceed it, we do usage-based pricing. I think that like, yeah, it's very dependent on your own user base and what they will do. But I will say monetization is something you should think about up front and design your agent around, because it's hard to walk back these promises.

Yeah, back there. I haven't heard you talk at all about hooks. And I'd be curious to hear your take on what's like, within there. Yeah, there's so much to talk about. Hooks are great. We do ship with hooks. Hooks are a way of doing deterministic verification, in particular, or inserting context. So we fire these hooks in the events, and you can register them in the agent SDK.

There's a guide on how to do that. Examples of things you might use hooks for is, for example, you can run it to verify a spreadsheet each time. You can also, let's say I'm working with an agent, and the agent is doing some spreadsheet operations, and the user has also changed the spreadsheet.

This is an interesting place to use a hook, because you could be like, hey, after every tool call, insert changes that the user has made. And so you're giving kind of live context changes in an interesting way. So yeah, I think there's more stuff in the docs about hooks. And I'm happy to talk about it afterwards as well.

Yeah, more questions? Yeah? Yeah. Yeah, sure. Yeah, so let's say you've done this prototyping and you found something that works. What I would do is I'd summarize a CLOUD.MD. Obviously, when I tried doing this one time, it didn't use my API directly, and it wrote JavaScript.

I should have been more specific in my CLOUD.MD to be like, hey, you should use this. Yeah, I think that's one thing. The second thing is, yeah, just summarize a CLOUD.MD, have the helper scripts that you need, and then write something like this agent.ts script to run what it does to you.

Yeah, more questions? Yeah, in the gray? Yeah, I put it up for money, and it keeps failing. So I'll put the script in there. It tries a couple times. Like, my SDK isn't very good, so I put it in. But then it tries twice, and then it's like, oh, here's your comparison table. But it's just because it's not using distribution.

Do you have any advice for that kind of problem? Yeah, this is a good question. And I think there is some messiness, right? I think one of the things, if an agent knows an answer, and you want to fight it to be like, OK, it's generation 9 now, and Venusaur's stats have changed, and there's this new character — this is hard, I actually think.

One of the ways of doing that is hooks. So you can say, for example, like, hey, don't return a response without writing a script. You can check that, you can give feedback like, "Please make sure you write a script. Please make sure you read this data right." And you can use hooks to give that feedback in the same way.

They call code. We have these rules like, make sure you read a file before you write to it, right? So add some determinism. It can definitely be — like I said, it's an art, you know. Sometimes, you know, yeah, maybe like riding a horse, I guess, probably. Yeah, and they're great.

Working like a 50 million-plus in my case, and so yeah, prep tool doesn't work really. No, I'm having to build like my own semantic indexing type thing to kind of help with that, right? Sure, any kind of add-in product — maybe think about how that can be more native to the product.

Like, you know, in a couple months the thing I'm writing is gonna go away, or like how — how do you guys think? Okay, your last question: in a couple months is you're thinking to go away generally, yes. About AI, yeah, I think that semantic search — this is a cloud code question where the agent has to create a question, but happy to answer it.

You know, there are trade-offs of semantic search. It's more brittle, I think. You have to like index and search, and it's not — the model is not trained on semantic search. And so I think that's sort of like a problem, like, you know, graph — it's trained on because it's like it's easy to do that.

But like semantic search you're implementing your bespoke query. For very large code bases, you know, we have lots of customers that work in large code bases. I think what I've seen is sort of like they just do good, clogged-up FDs you start in, you know.

Make sure you start in the directory you want. Have good verification steps and hooks and links and things like that. And so, you know, that's what we do. We don't have, you know, a custom — we don't go through clogged code, right? So, yeah.

Okay. Yeah, last question. We have to close unfortunately actually. You.
